scenario: A FEEL expression must transform a list of products by extracting just their name field: products = [{name: "A", price: 10}, {name: "B", price: 20}] → ["A", "B"].
question: Which FEEL expression performs this projection?

options.text: name is valid FEEL path expression shortcut syntax. In FEEL, applying a dot-path to a list of contexts returns a new list containing the named field from each context element. This is equivalent to the for-loop projection and produces the same result list. FEEL path projection on lists is a concise alternative to explicit for-loops for simple field extraction. Both forms are standard FEEL -- this is the path-access approach that many find more readable than explicit iteration.

map(products, "name") uses a function-call syntax absent from FEEL. The FEEL language specification defines no map() built-in. name. Calling a non-existent map() function causes a FEEL evaluation error. map() or Haskell map may expect this syntax but must use the FEEL for-loop form.

name is JSONPath syntax, not FEEL syntax. JSONPath uses the wildcard [] selector to project a field across array elements. name) for the same operation. JSONPath expressions in FEEL contexts cause parsing errors -- the [*] is not valid FEEL syntax. These are distinct expression languages with different grammars despite superficially similar goals.

name iterates over the products list and constructs a new list containing only the name field from each element. This is the standard FEEL projection syntax: for <variable> in <list> return <expression>. It produces a list of name strings corresponding to each product context in the input list. This is one of two valid FEEL approaches to list projection, the other being path expression dot-notation on a list. ###

a.text: Incorrect. Canonical Camunda FEEL projection uses the for a in b return c loop syntax (per feel-control-flow.md:35-53): for x in [1,2,3] return x * 2 → [2,4,6]. Equivalently for our case: for p in products return p.name → ["A", "B"]. The bracket comprehension form [item in list : expr] does not exist in Camunda's FEEL.

b.text: Incorrect. products.name on a list of context values is a Camunda-extension path-projection convenience that produces the same result. Less explicit than the for loop; works only when every element has the same field name.

c.text: Incorrect. No map() function in Camunda FEEL list functions (per feel-built-in-functions-list.md -- count/min/max/sum/mean/median/stddev/mode/all/any/product but no map).

d.text: Correct. FEEL is not JSONPath; [*] wildcard accessor doesn't exist.

explanation: FEEL is not JSONPath; [*] wildcard accessor doesn't exist.
